schools.summary: The opening and chain of disclosure: 'the Revelation (apokalypsis — an unveiling, disclosure) of Jesus Christ, which God gave unto him, to shew unto his servants things which must shortly come to pass (en tachei — quickly, soon); and he sent and signified (esemanen — showed by signs, symbols) it by his angel unto his servant John' — the book an unveiling of things hidden, belonging to Christ, given to him by the Father, to be made known to his servants (the divine chain: God to Christ to the angel to John); 'shortly come to pass' read by some as imminence (the events soon to begin) and by others as swiftness once begun; the revelation given through an angel and in signs/symbols, the symbolic character of the book marked from the outset (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

Victorinus and Bede: the Revelation, given by the Father to the Son and signified by his angel to John, is conveyed in signs and symbols ('signified' marking the figurative manner); the unveiling of Christ to his servants, delivered through angelic mediation, is to be read as a symbolic prophecy of the things that must come to pass.

themes: The Revelation of Jesus Christ, which God gave unto him, to shew unto his servants things which must shortly come to pass
he sent and signified it by his angel unto his servant John
the Revelation given by God through Christ
the symbolic unveiling signified by an angel

schools.summary: John's role as witness: 'who bare record (emartyresen — witnessed, testified) of the word of God, and of the testimony of Jesus Christ, and of all things that he saw' — John faithfully testifying to all he received (the word of God, the testimony of Jesus, and the visions shown him); 'the word of God' and 'the testimony of Jesus Christ' the substance of his witness (the divine message and the witness Christ bore, or the witness borne to Christ, the same phrases recurring as the ground of John's exile, v.9); 'all things that he saw' the entire content of the visions, John the faithful recorder writing down what was shown him (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: Who bare record of the word of God, and of the testimony of Jesus Christ, and of all things that he saw
John bearing record of the word and testimony of Christ
the faithful witness of the apostle
the word, the testimony, and the visions

schools.summary: The first beatitude of the book: 'blessed is he that readeth (the one who reads aloud, perhaps in the assembly), and they that hear (the congregation) the words of this prophecy, and keep (tereo — observe, obey, treasure) those things which are written therein: for the time is at hand (near)' — a blessing pronounced on the public reader and the hearers of the book (the reading and hearing of the prophecy bringing blessing), but belonging especially to those who keep what is written (not mere hearing but obeying, the book not for curiosity but for obedience, the blessing resting on doing not only hearing); the reason being that the time of the things foretold is near (the imminence, read as the events soon to begin or near in God's reckoning, pressing the call to heed) — Barnes, Robertson, Henry.

themes: Blessed is he that readeth, and they that hear the words of this prophecy, and keep those things which are written therein
for the time is at hand
the beatitude on those who read, hear, and keep the prophecy
the blessing resting on obedience

schools.summary: Gill (with the traditional Trinitarian reading): 'grace be unto you, and peace, from him which is, and which was, and which is to come (the eternal, unchanging God, the Father, the everlasting I AM in past, present, and future); and from the seven Spirits which are before his throne' — 'the seven Spirits' being the Holy Spirit, represented under a sevenfold figure (the fullness and perfection of the one Spirit, sevenfold in his operations and gifts, cf. Isaiah 11:2; Revelation 4:5; 5:6); the placement in the Trinitarian salutation between the Father (v.4) and Christ (v.5) strongly favouring the Holy Spirit, the Spirit named with the Father and the Son as the joint source of grace and peace.

Barnes inclines to seven angels: the 'seven Spirits' the seven principal angels (the 'angels of the presence' that stand before the throne), citing the difficulty of calling the Holy Spirit 'seven Spirits' (how could God be described, with the article, as 'before his throne'?) and the parallel with 'the seven angels which stood before God' (8:2) and the seven lamps before the throne (4:5); the angelic reading taking the seven Spirits as created beings ministering before God, though Barnes acknowledges the Trinitarian objection.

debates.question: Who or what are 'the seven Spirits which are before his throne'?

sides.position: The Holy Spirit, represented under a sevenfold figure (the fullness/perfection of the one Spirit) — the Trinitarian salutation
Seven angels (the principal 'angels of the presence') that stand before the throne

themes: John to the seven churches which are in Asia: Grace be unto you, and peace, from him which is, and which was, and which is to come
and from the seven Spirits which are before his throne
the greeting from the eternal God and the seven Spirits
the crux of the seven Spirits

schools.summary: The threefold description of Christ and the doxology: 'and from Jesus Christ, who is the faithful (true, trustworthy) witness (martys, who bore true testimony to God's truth even unto death, the model of faithful witness for the persecuted churches), and the first begotten (prototokos — firstborn) of the dead (the first to rise to immortal life, the pledge of the resurrection), and the prince (archon — ruler) of the kings of the earth (the sovereign over all earthly rulers). Unto him that loved us, and washed (or loosed, lysanti, by a textual variant) us from our sins in (by) his own blood' — the three titles spanning Christ's faithful witness, his resurrection, and his universal dominion; the mention of Christ breaking into praise, his redeeming love and atoning blood the ground of the doxology (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

Cyril: the doxology rises to him who loved us and washed us from our sins in his own blood — Christ's redeeming love and cleansing blood calling forth adoration; the faithful witness, risen firstborn, and ruler of earth's kings is the Redeemer whose atoning blood frees his people from sin, the fit object of glory and dominion.

themes: Jesus Christ, who is the faithful witness, and the first begotten of the dead, and the prince of the kings of the earth
Unto him that loved us, and washed us from our sins in his own blood
the threefold title of Christ and the doxology
the faithful, risen, reigning Redeemer

schools.summary: The result of redemption and the doxology: 'and hath made us kings and priests (basileian, hiereis — a kingdom, priests; a royal priesthood) unto God and his Father; to him be glory and dominion for ever and ever. Amen' — Christ having made the redeemed kings and priests to God (the dignity of believers, raised to royal and priestly standing, cf. 1 Peter 2:9; Exodus 19:6); believers sharing Christ's royalty (they reign with him) and his priesthood (they offer spiritual sacrifices and have access to God), exalted to this double dignity; the doxology ascribing to Christ eternal glory and dominion (power), the praise that his love and redemption evoke sealed with 'Amen' (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: hath made us kings and priests unto God and his Father
to him be glory and dominion for ever and ever. Amen
Christ made us kings and priests, with the doxology of glory and dominion
the royal priesthood of the redeemed

schools.summary: The announcement of the second coming: 'behold, he cometh with clouds; and every eye shall see him, and they also which pierced him: and all kindreds of the earth shall wail because of him. Even so, Amen' — the great theme of the book announced, Christ coming with clouds (the symbol of divine majesty and judgment, cf. Daniel 7:13; Matthew 24:30); the coming visible and universal, beheld by all including 'they which pierced him' (those who crucified him, or those responsible, the Jews and all sinners); at his coming all the tribes of the earth wailing/mourning (whether in penitence or in terror at the judgment, the universal lament at the appearing of the Judge), 'Even so, Amen' sealing the certainty (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: Behold, he cometh with clouds; and every eye shall see him, and they also which pierced him
all kindreds of the earth shall wail because of him. Even so, Amen
the announcement of Christ's coming with clouds, seen by all
the universal, visible coming of the Judge

schools.summary: The divine self-declaration: 'I am Alpha and Omega (the first and last letters of the Greek alphabet), the beginning and the ending, saith the Lord, which is, and which was, and which is to come, the Almighty (pantokrator — the all-powerful, ruler of all)' — the speaker declaring himself the first and the last, the beginning and end of all (the title of eternity and all-encompassing being, spoken by 'the Lord God' and applied also to Christ, v.11; 22:13); the eternal, unchanging God existing in past, present, and future (the title repeated from v.4); and the omnipotent God whose almighty power undergirds the certainty of all the book foretells — the title combining eternity (Alpha and Omega, is/was/is to come) with omnipotence (the Almighty) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: I am Alpha and Omega, the beginning and the ending, saith the Lord
which is, and which was, and which is to come, the Almighty
the divine self-declaration: Alpha and Omega, the Almighty
eternity joined with omnipotence

schools.summary: John's humble self-identification and exile: 'I John, who also am your brother (not lording it over them), and companion (synkoinonos — sharer, fellow-partaker) in tribulation (affliction, persecution), and in the kingdom and patience (hypomone — patient endurance) of Jesus Christ, was in the isle that is called Patmos (a rocky island in the Aegean, a place of banishment), for the word of God, and for the testimony of Jesus Christ' — John naming himself simply as a brother and fellow-sufferer, sharing the churches' trials not above them; tribulation, kingdom, and patient endurance going together (the believer sharing Christ's sufferings, his kingdom, and the patience that endures until the kingdom comes); John exiled to Patmos on account of his faithful preaching and witness, his suffering for the gospel the occasion of the vision (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).

themes: I John, who also am your brother, and companion in tribulation, and in the kingdom and patience of Jesus Christ
was in the isle that is called Patmos, for the word of God, and for the testimony of Jesus Christ
John on Patmos, a brother and companion in tribulation
exiled for his faithful witness

schools.summary: The prophetic ecstasy and the day: 'I was (egenomen — came to be) in the Spirit (in an ecstatic, prophetic condition, under the Spirit's special influence, abstracted from ordinary consciousness, cf. Acts 10:10; 22:17; 2 Corinthians 12:2) on the Lord's day, and heard behind me a great (loud) voice, as of a trumpet (clear, loud, and commanding, the voice of Christ, vv.12-13).' Barnes, Ellicott, and Robertson (with Gill, Clarke) take 'the Lord's day (kyriake hemera)' as the first day of the week, Sunday — the Christian Lord's Day, so called in honour of Christ's resurrection (the same adjective kyriakos used of 'the Lord's Supper,' 1 Corinthians 11:20; the day called the Lord's day by Ignatius and the early fathers, distinguished from the Jewish Sabbath). Ellicott: 'there is no ground whatever for the futurist interpretation that this expression refers to the Day of the Lord'; Robertson: it 'has no reference to hemera kyriou (the day of judgment).'

debates.question: What is 'the Lord's day' on which John was in the Spirit?

sides.position: The first day of the week, Sunday — the Christian Lord's Day, in honour of Christ's resurrection (the consensus)
The eschatological 'day of the Lord' (the day of judgment), into which John was transported in vision (the futurist reading)
holders: the futurist interpreters (named and rejected by Ellicott and Robertson)

themes: I was in the Spirit on the Lord's day
heard behind me a great voice, as of a trumpet
John in the Spirit on the Lord's day
the crux of the Lord's day

schools.summary: The command to write: 'saying, I am Alpha and Omega, the first and the last: and, What thou seest, write in a book (scroll), and send it unto the seven churches which are in Asia; unto Ephesus, and unto Smyrna, and unto Pergamos, and unto Thyatira, and unto Sardis, and unto Philadelphia, and unto Laodicea' — the voice declaring itself (Christ, as vv.17-18 show) with the divine title of v.8 ('the first and the last,' applied to Christ, affirming his deity, though many texts omit the opening clause, the title recurring in v.17); John to record the visions and send the written account to the seven churches (the book a circular letter to all seven); the seven actual churches in proconsular Asia named in the geographical order of a circuit, to each a particular message following (chs. 2-3) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: I am Alpha and Omega, the first and the last: and, What thou seest, write in a book
send it unto the seven churches which are in Asia; unto Ephesus, and unto Smyrna...
the command to write to the seven churches by name
the circular letter to the seven churches of Asia

schools.summary: John's response and the first vision: 'and I turned to see the voice (the one whose voice it was) that spake with me. And being turned, I saw seven golden candlesticks (lychnias — lampstands)' — John turning toward the voice and seeing seven golden lampstands (the first object of the vision); the lampstands golden (precious, costly), explained (v.20) as the seven churches (the church a lampstand, holding up the light of the gospel in the world); the golden lampstands representing the churches as bearers of light (their office to give light, to hold forth the word, the gold marking their preciousness to Christ, the seven the completeness of the church) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Henry.

themes: I turned to see the voice that spake with me. And being turned, I saw seven golden candlesticks
the vision of the seven golden candlesticks
the churches as golden lampstands bearing light
precious to Christ, set to bear the gospel light

schools.summary: The central figure: 'and in the midst of the seven candlesticks one like unto the Son of man (homoion huion anthropou, the title from Daniel 7:13), clothed with a garment down to the foot (poderes — a long robe, the dress of the high priest and of dignity), and girt about the paps (the breast) with a golden girdle (a symbol of royal and priestly office)' — Christ standing in the midst of the churches (the title 'Son of man' marking him as the glorified, exalted one of Daniel's vision, walking among his churches); 'in the midst' signifying his presence with and among his churches (not absent but watching, sustaining, and judging them); the long robe and golden girdle marking his priestly and kingly dignity (Christ appearing as the royal High Priest of his churches) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

Cyril: Christ, the Son of man, stands in the midst of the lampstands clothed in the long priestly robe and golden girdle — present among his churches as their royal High Priest, ever in their midst to sustain, watch over, and judge them; the dress marks his priestly and kingly office, the glorified Lord walking among his own.

themes: in the midst of the seven candlesticks one like unto the Son of man
clothed with a garment down to the foot, and girt about the paps with a golden girdle
the glorified Son of man among the candlesticks
Christ the royal High Priest present with his churches

schools.summary: The symbolic description: 'his head and his hairs were white like wool, as white as snow; and his eyes were as a flame of fire' — the whiteness of his head and hair (drawn from Daniel 7:9, the Ancient of Days) signifying eternity, purity, and the venerable majesty of age (the glorified Christ sharing the appearance of the Ancient of Days, the dazzling whiteness marking his holiness and eternal, unchanging being, the application of the Ancient-of-Days imagery to Christ affirming his deity and eternity); and his eyes blazing like fire, symbolizing his penetrating, all-searching knowledge and his consuming judgment (nothing hidden from his sight, the flaming eyes searching the churches, cf. 2:18, 23) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: his head and his hairs were white like wool, as white as snow; and his eyes were as a flame of fire
the white head and flaming eyes of the glorified Christ
the eternity and purity of the Ancient of Days
the all-searching eyes of penetrating judgment

schools.summary: The further features: 'and his feet like unto fine brass (chalkolibanon — burnished or glowing brass/bronze), as if they burned in a furnace; and his voice as the sound of many waters' — his feet shining like brass glowing in a furnace (the symbol of strength, stability, and the splendour of his glorified body, some seeing the glowing feet as the symbol of treading down enemies in judgment, signifying firmness and the consuming purity of judgment, Christ walking among the churches with feet of judgment); and his voice mighty, full, and majestic like the roar of a great cataract or the sea (the overwhelming power and majesty of his speech, cf. Ezekiel 43:2) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: his feet like unto fine brass, as if they burned in a furnace; and his voice as the sound of many waters
the feet of burnished brass and voice of many waters
strength and the splendour of judgment
the mighty, majestic voice

schools.summary: The climax of the vision: 'and he had in his right hand seven stars: and out of his mouth went a sharp twoedged sword (rhomphaia — a long sword): and his countenance was as the sun shineth in his strength (in full, noonday brightness)' — Christ holding seven stars in his right hand (explained, v.20, as the angels/ministers of the seven churches, the symbol of his protecting power and ownership, holding and upholding them); the sword proceeding from his mouth the word of Christ, sharp and piercing, dividing and judging (cf. Hebrews 4:12; Isaiah 49:2, his word both saving and smiting, the sword of judgment against his enemies); and his face blazing like the sun at full strength (the unbearable, dazzling glory of the glorified Christ, cf. the Transfiguration, Matthew 17:2, the splendour that overwhelms the beholder) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: in his right hand seven stars: and out of his mouth went a sharp twoedged sword
his countenance was as the sun shineth in his strength
the seven stars, the sword of his mouth, and the shining countenance
Christ holder of the churches, judge by his word, dazzling in glory

schools.summary: John's prostration and Christ's reassurance: 'and when I saw him, I fell at his feet as (one) dead. And he laid his right hand upon me, saying unto me, Fear not; I am the first and the last' — the overwhelming glory of the vision striking John down (the natural effect of beholding the divine majesty being prostration and faintness, cf. Ezekiel 1:28; Daniel 8:17; 10:9, the apostle overcome); the same Christ whose glory overwhelmed him laying his right hand (the hand that held the stars) on him and reassuring him with the tender 'Fear not' (the majestic Lord also the gracious comforter, calming the terror); the reassurance resting on the divine title 'I am the first and the last' (from Isaiah 44:6; 48:12, applied to Christ, affirming his deity and eternity, the everlasting one not to be feared by his own, his eternal being the ground of the believer's confidence) — Barnes, Robertson, Henry.

themes: when I saw him, I fell at his feet as dead. And he laid his right hand upon me, saying... Fear not
I am the first and the last
John falling as dead and Christ's reassuring Fear not
the eternal Christ both awe-inspiring and gracious

schools.summary: Christ the living one and his authority: 'I am he that liveth, and was dead; and, behold, I am alive for evermore, Amen; and have the keys of hell (hades, the unseen world of the dead) and of death' — Christ the living one who died (became dead) and now lives forever (alive unto the ages of the ages, his death and triumphant resurrection the ground of his reassurance and authority, death behind him forever, his endless life securing the life of his people, 'Amen' sealing the certainty); and holding the keys of hades and death (the symbol of authority and control over death and the realm of the dead, the risen Lord having power over death and hades, opening and shutting as he wills, the comfort that the believer's death and destiny are in his hands) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: I am he that liveth, and was dead; and, behold, I am alive for evermore
have the keys of hell and of death
Christ the living one who holds the keys of death and hell
the risen Lord master of death and the grave

schools.summary: The comprehensive charge giving a threefold outline: 'write the things which thou hast seen (the vision just described, ch. 1), and the things which are (the present state of the churches, chs. 2-3), and the things which shall be hereafter (ha mellei genesthai meta tauta — after these, the future things, chs. 4-22)' — the command structuring the book: the vision seen, the present, and the future; 'the things which thou hast seen' the vision of the glorified Christ just received; 'the things which are' the present condition of the seven churches (addressed in the letters), and 'the things which shall be hereafter' the future events unveiled in the rest of the book; many taking this verse as the key to the book's structure, dividing it into the vision, the messages to the churches, and the prophetic visions of things to come (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: Write the things which thou hast seen, and the things which are, and the things which shall be hereafter
the threefold command structuring the book
the vision, the present churches, and the future
the key to the book's arrangement

schools.summary: Christ's own explanation of the symbols: 'the seven candlesticks (lampstands)... are the seven churches' (the lampstands the seven churches, holding up the gospel light); 'the seven stars are the angels (angeloi — messengers) of the seven churches.' Barnes and Robertson (with Gill) take the 'angels' as the human ministers/pastors of the churches — the presiding minister or chief teacher of each church (each letter addressed to 'the angel' of a church being addressed to its minister, who represents the congregation, held in Christ's right hand as the symbol of his protecting ownership). Barnes rejects the prelatical-bishop reading (no evidence of a single bishop over a group of churches, and if 'angel' meant 'bishop' there would be seven bishops at once) and the 'messengers sent to John' reading (Lord's view, since the letters are sent TO the angel, not by him); 'angel' applies as well to the pastor of a single church.

Alternative readings of the 'angels': some take them as literal angels — the guardian angels of the churches (the word 'angel' in its usual sense of celestial messenger, the angel watching over or representing each congregation before God); others take them as personifications or representatives of the churches themselves (the angel standing for the church, its collective character or spirit), the letters thus addressed to the church through its 'angel.'

debates.question: Who are the 'angels' of the seven churches?

sides.position: The human ministers/pastors (the messengers, presiding teachers) of the churches
Literal angels — the guardian angels of the churches (or personifications of the churches themselves)

themes: The seven stars are the angels of the seven churches: and the seven candlesticks... are the seven churches
the explanation of the stars and candlesticks
the crux of the angels of the churches
the ministers of the churches held in Christ's hand
